statement: The Minotaur was a creature with the body of a man and the head of a bull, imprisoned in the Labyrinth of Crete built by the master craftsman Daedalus.
quizQuestion: Where was the Minotaur imprisoned?
explanation: The Minotaur (Greek: Minōtauros — 'Bull of Minos') was the offspring of Queen Pasiphae of Crete and a divine bull. statement: Theseus slew the Minotaur and found his way out of the Labyrinth using a thread given to him by the princess Ariadne, who had fallen in love with him.
quizQuestion: How did Theseus navigate out of the Labyrinth?
explanation: Theseus, prince of Athens, volunteered to enter the Labyrinth as one of the tributes. Princess Ariadne, daughter of King Minos, fell in love with him and gave him a ball of thread (the 'clew of Ariadne'). He tied one end at the entrance, slew the Minotaur with his bare hands (or a sword), then followed the thread back out. The English word 'clue' derives from this story.
wowFactor: The word 'clue' literally comes from Ariadne's thread — a 'clew' was an old word for a ball of yarn, and finding your way out became finding a clue.

statement: Anubis was the ancient Egyptian jackal-headed god of the dead who presided over mummification and guided souls through the afterlife.
quizQuestion: What animal head does Anubis have?
explanation: Anubis (ancient Egyptian: Inpw) is one of the oldest and most iconic deities in the Egyptian pantheon. He is depicted with the head of a black jackal and the body of a man. Jackals were associated with death because they were scavengers often seen near cemeteries. Anubis was the patron of embalming, protector of tombs, and guide of souls to the Hall of Two Truths for judgment.
wowFactor: Ancient Egyptians chose the jackal as Anubis's form specifically because jackals roamed cemeteries — they transformed a threatening scavenger into a divine guardian.

statement: Anubis weighed the hearts of the deceased against a feather of Ma'at — those whose hearts were heavier than the feather were devoured by the monster Ammit.
quizQuestion: What did Anubis weigh hearts against in the afterlife?
explanation: In the Hall of Two Truths, Anubis placed the heart of the deceased on one side of a great scale and the Feather of Ma'at (representing truth and justice) on the other. If the heart was lighter than or equal to the feather, the soul was deemed righteous and passed to Aaru (the Field of Reeds). If heavier (weighed down by sin), the heart was immediately devoured by Ammit — a hybrid monster — and the soul ceased to exist.
wowFactor: Failing the Egyptian afterlife judgment wasn't hell — it was total annihilation. Your soul was eaten and you simply stopped existing forever.

explanation: After the Olympians defeated the Titans, Zeus, Poseidon, and Hades drew lots to divide the world. Zeus drew the sky, Poseidon the sea, and Hades drew the underworld. Although Hades rules the realm of the dead, he is not a death god in the sense of causing death — Thanatos handled death itself. Hades rarely left his realm and was considered stern but fair.

statement: Hades abducted Persephone and made her queen of the underworld — her mother Demeter's grief caused crops to wither, explaining the origin of winter.
quizQuestion: Who became queen of Hades's underworld?
explanation: Hades abducted Persephone (daughter of Demeter, goddess of the harvest) and made her his wife and queen. Demeter's grief caused all plants to die. Zeus negotiated: Persephone would return, but having eaten pomegranate seeds in the underworld, she was bound to return there for part of each year. This explains the seasons — when Persephone is below, Demeter grieves and it is winter; when she returns, spring blooms.
wowFactor: The ancient Greeks explained all of winter as Demeter's depression — every year, she mourns her daughter's descent into the underworld.

Hades lent it to Perseus to help slay Medusa, and Athena wore it during the Trojan War to move unseen on the battlefield.

statement: Brahma is the Hindu god of creation and the first member of the Trimurti (trinity), depicted with four heads facing the four directions and four arms.

explanation: Brahma (not to be confused with Brahman, the absolute) is the Hindu god of creation. He is part of the Trimurti: Brahma (creation), Vishnu (preservation), and Shiva (destruction). His four heads are said to have arisen from his wish to observe his creation from all directions. Each head recites one of the four Vedas. statement: Set (also Seth) was the Egyptian god of chaos, storms, and the desert — he murdered his brother Osiris and scattered the body to prevent his resurrection.
quizQuestion: What did Set do to his brother Osiris?
acceptableAnswers: Killed and cut up his body
explanation: Set was one of the major Egyptian deities associated with chaos, storms, the desert, violence, and foreigners. In the Osiris myth, Set murdered Osiris — in some versions he lured Osiris into a coffin, sealed it, and threw it in the Nile; in others he dismembered the body into 14 (or 42) pieces scattered across Egypt. Isis gathered the pieces and, with Anubis's help, reassembled and mummified Osiris, who then became king of the dead.
wowFactor: Set cut Osiris into 14 pieces and scattered them across Egypt — Isis found every piece except one, and the world's first mummy was made from what remained.

statement: Set battled Horus (Osiris's son) for eighty years for rule of Egypt — Set tore out Horus's eye and Horus tore off Set's testicles before the gods declared Horus the rightful king.
quizQuestion: How long did Set and Horus battle for Egypt?
explanation: The Contendings of Horus and Set is an Egyptian mythological text describing an 80-year divine tribunal and battle for the throne of Egypt. Set, as Osiris's brother, and Horus, as Osiris's son, both claimed the right to rule. The conflict involved trials, battles, and deliberation by the gods. Ultimately the gods ruled in favor of Horus. The Eye of Horus — a powerful symbol of protection — originated from the eye Set tore out during combat.
wowFactor: The Eye of Horus, still used as a symbol of protection and good luck today, commemorates the eye torn out during an 80-year divine war for Egypt's throne.
